data.title: Crude oil posts biggest weekly gains since October as market watches Iran
data.description:
Crude oil futures ended little changed Friday but wrapped up their largest weekly gains since October, as the buildup of U.S. military forces in the Middle East heightened concerns that strikes against Iran could occur soon, potentially disrupting global crude supply.

An escalation between the U.S. and Iran would place ~1.5M bbl/day of Iranian oil exports at risk and spark worries over broader Persian Gulf oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

"It's really all about Iran right now" regarding oil prices, Again Capital's John Kilduff said in a note. "The market had priced in a lot of geopolitical risk on Iran, but it's difficult to quantify the market at this point. The question is if there is action against Iran, what will the Iranians do?"

Oil stabilized Friday after President Trump told reporters that Iran wants to make a deal, possibly a signal that he may now be leaning toward diplomacy rather than military action in the region.

Prices also have been lifted by supply disruptions elsewhere, including a significant loss of crude exports from Kazakhstan due to damage at the Caspian Pipeline Consortium, although flows are expected to recover in the coming months.

The massive winter storm that slammed much of the U.S. this week has supported demand for heating fuels and disrupted production on the Gulf Coast and elsewhere, but the overall fundamentals outlook remains bearish due to growing oversupply.

"The oil market remains well supplied, although the oversupply is not quite as high as initially assumed thanks to supply disruptions and slightly stronger demand," Commerzbank's Barbara Lambrecht said in a note. "Inventory data next week should provide insight into the extent of the disruptions in the U.S."

Key OPEC+ members are set to meet virtually on Sunday, with no changes to production policy expected for now.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for March delivery closed -0.3% to $65.21/bbl, snapping a three-session winning streak, and front-month March Brent crude (CO1:COM) fell $0.02 to $70.69/bbl; Nymex and Brent rallied 6.8% and 7.3%, respectively, for the week, and jumped 13.6% and 16.2%, respectively, in January.

U.S. natural gas (NG1:COM) rose for the eighth time in nine sessions as continuing frigid weather keeps near-term demand expectations high while production has not fully recovered from major freeze-offs earlier in the week; front-month Nymex gas for March delivery settled Friday +11.1% at $4.354/MMBtu, while surging 20.6% for the week and 18.1% for the month.

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), finished the week +3.8%.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Exxon, Chevron see hope for Venezuela but sweeping changes needed
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) offered insights into their perspectives about Venezuela during earnings conference calls on Friday, even as neither company announced long-term investment commitments despite ​President Trump's push to convince U.S. oil companies to pump $100B into the country's energy sector.

Exxon (XOM) CEO ‌Darren Woods touted his company's technological capability to potentially extract Venezuela's expensive heavy crude for a lower cost, while Chevron (CVX) CEO Mike Wirth said his company would process more Venezuelan crude through its refineries in the U.S., and both bosses said they wanted to see strong legal frameworks and a stable political environment before making decisions about long-term projects.

Woods, who called Venezuela "uninvestable" at a White House meeting earlier this month, said he believes the Trump administration is working to stabilize the country, boost its economy and transition into a democratic government, and said Exxon (XOM) offers technological capabilities to pump Venezuela's heavy crude, because it produces similar crude in Canada.

"We think we bring an advantaged approach that will lead to lower-cost production [and] higher recovery, Woods said on Exxon's (XOM) earnings call. "That's the opportunity set for us that will play out, maybe over time," adding that the company still plans to send a technical team to assess the situation in Venezuela.

Chevron (CVX) could increase Venezuelan heavy crude flows in its U.S. refining system, but that it is too early to determine the company's long-term outlook on the country, CEO Mike Wirth said on his company's earnings call, noting the company currently processes ~50K bbl/day at its Pascagoula refinery in Mississippi and could run an additional 100K bbl/day across its Gulf Coast refineries and on the West Coast at El Segundo in California.

"There will be a number of signposts that we'll be watching," Wirth said. "It will have to compete in our portfolio versus attractive investments in many other parts of the world. With the right changes, we certainly could see our operations in the footprint expand in Venezuela."

Exxon (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) both reported their smallest annual profits since 2021, pressured by a growing glut of crude that has weighed on prices.

data.title: BE Resources announces private placement of 2.6M common shares
data.description:
• BE Resources (BER.H:CA) announced on Friday that it is proposing to complete a non-brokered private placement of 2,600,000 common shares at $0.05 each to raise aggregate gross proceeds up to $130,000.
  • The offering remains subject to various closing conditions, including the approval of the TSX Venture Exchange, the company said. 
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Cannabist Company enters forbearance agreement with senior noteholders
data.description:
• The Cannabist Company Holdings (Cboe CA: CBST) (OTCQB: CBSTF) said on Friday it has entered into a forbearance agreement with noteholders representing more than 75% of its senior secured notes due in 2028.
  • The agreement follows the company’s decision not to make interest payments due on December 31, 2025, which became an event of default after the expiration of a 30-day grace period.
  • Under the forbearance agreement, the noteholders agreed to refrain from exercising remedies related to the missed payments until February 17, 2026.
  • The company said it is continuing discussions with noteholders regarding options to address its liquidity needs, including potential asset sales or other strategic or financial transactions.
  • CBSTF closed -8.31% at $0.043.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: First Business projects 10% annual growth in loans, deposits, and revenue for 2026 while boosting dividend 17%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Business Financial Services, Inc. (FBIZ) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Corey Chambas, CEO, stated that "First Business Bank finished 2025 with another outstanding quarter. Our team continued to produce high-quality growth, particularly on the deposit side. Core net interest margin remained resilient and our revenue streams were diversified and strong." Chambas highlighted that "Private Wealth business continued to expand, delivering record and significant annuity-like fee income," and that "pretax pre-provision earnings grew nearly 15% over 2024, return on average tangible common equity was over 15% for the year." He also reported that "tangible book value per share grew 14% from a year ago" and that "2025 EPS grew 14% over 2024, exceeding our long-term annual goal of 10% earnings growth."

  • Chambas announced a "17% increase to our quarterly cash dividend" and emphasized the company’s track record of double-digit growth, aiming to continue this in 2026.

  • David Seiler, President & COO, addressed an isolated credit situation: "During the quarter, we downgraded $20.4 million of CRE loans related to a single Wisconsin-based borrower with total loans outstanding of $29.7 million." He explained that this was due to the borrower's inability to advance parcels for multifamily development, but noted, "land value appraisals exceed the carrying value of the loans. As such, a specific reserve was not recorded."

  • Seiler pointed out that "Private Wealth generated a record $3.8 million of fee income, up 11% year-over-year" and that "service charges were up nearly 20% year-over-year." He also noted that "core deposit balances were up 12% from both the linked and prior year quarters."

  • Brian Spielmann, CFO, stated, "Fourth quarter net interest margin declined by 15 basis points to 3.53%, reflecting 10 basis points of compression from the nonaccrual interest reversal on the downgraded CRE nonperforming loan. Excluding this, net interest margin would have measured 3.63%." He added, "On a full year basis, net interest margin remained relatively stable, declining 2 basis points from 3.66% in 2024 to 3.64% in 2025."

  • Spielmann explained an accounting change: "In the fourth quarter, we reclassified $904,000 out of noninterest expense and into other noninterest income to net against the related revenue. Excluding this reclassification, income from partnership investments decreased $383,000 to $477,000 during the fourth quarter."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated a target of 10% annual growth in net interest income, loans, deposits, and revenue for 2026. Spielmann said, "Our target range for net interest margin is unchanged. Our current outlook supports this in tandem with double-digit annual loan, deposit and revenue growth."

  • Seiler forecasted, "We would expect to see growth rebound to our typical double-digit pace in 2026," noting strong loan demand and expanding pipelines.

  • Spielmann added, "We expect 2026 compensation levels to grow a bit more than in 2025" and confirmed, "Our 2025 effective tax rate of 16.8% was within our expected annual range of 16% to 18%, and we continue to believe this range is appropriate looking forward."

Financial Results

  • Chambas highlighted that "EPS growth is perhaps the most universal metric across industries, and our track record is outstanding."

  • Seiler noted, "Loan balances grew about $39 million or 5% annualized during the quarter and $261 million or 8% over the same period last year."

  • Seiler emphasized, "Private Wealth generated a record $3.8 million of fee income, up 11% year-over-year," and "service charges were up nearly 20% year-over-year."

  • Spielmann reported, "Fourth quarter net interest margin declined by 15 basis points to 3.53%."

  • Seiler stated, "Net charge-offs totaled $2.5 million and were primarily from previously reserved equipment finance loans."

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about CRE appraisals and loan-to-value. Chambas responded, "Most of the appraisals, we just got several in just now at the end of the year... overall loan-to-value across those seven properties is 72% on the LTV."

  • Tamayo queried about credit costs. Chambas clarified, "The charge-offs that we had for the quarter were not related to this. So based on those appraisals, we didn't have to take even any reserves on this."

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about the timeline for resolution of the problem loan. Chambas said, "We do think because there are multiple pieces of real estate here that there can be shorter-term progress potentially with some pieces of this, even in the very near term."

  • Rulis asked about equipment finance balances. Chambas answered, "We were at $21 million at the end of the quarter. And I think that went down about $20 million over the course of the year."

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Inquired about margin starting point and loan mix. Spielmann replied, "When you adjust for the nonaccrual interest in Q4, that resets us at 3.63%. And with that mix that we're seeing in the pipelines, we feel like it's a great place to be and within our range of 3.60% to 3.65%."

  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Asked about expense growth. Spielmann answered, "Compensation is going to increase a little bit more than 7.5% this year, there's not much left for the rest of the expenses, and that's consistent with our approach to generating annual positive operating leverage."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on credit quality, problem loan resolution timing, and fee income guidance, with a slightly cautious but constructive tone.

  • Management maintained a confident and positive tone, emphasizing strong execution and the ability to sustain growth, though acknowledging isolated credit issues and competitive deposit pricing. Chambas stated, "We know how to execute to achieve our double-digit growth mandate, and we aim to continue doing so in 2026 and beyond."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, the management tone remains confident and consistent, while analysts' questions showed a slightly increased focus on credit risk and loan growth sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remains consistent, reiterating double-digit growth targets in loans, deposits, and revenue.

  • The isolated credit event in CRE this quarter contrasts with the previous quarter's stable asset quality.

  • Loan growth moderated in Q4 compared to stronger Q3, due to higher CRE payoffs, but management expects rebound in 2026.

  • Fee income drivers shifted from unusually high swap and SBIC income last quarter to record Private Wealth and service charges this quarter.

  • Management's tone and confidence in sustaining growth is unchanged, though analyst focus has shifted more toward credit quality and payoff trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management addressed a $20.4 million CRE loan downgrade related to a single borrower, emphasizing that the issue is isolated and collateral value exceeds carrying value.

  • Seiler reported, "Outside of the new and isolated nonaccrual relationship, the balance of our portfolio continues to perform as expected, and we have no areas of particular concern."

  • Spielmann noted, "Deposit pricing pressure has eased modestly since the Fed began cutting, the cost of acquiring a new deposit client remains extremely competitive."

  • Analysts expressed concerns about the duration and impact of credit resolution and future payoff activity in CRE.

Final Takeaway

First Business Financial Services closed 2025 with robust profitability, highlighted by double-digit annual growth in key metrics, a 17% dividend increase, and continued execution on its long-term growth mandate. Management remains confident in sustaining 10% annual growth in loans, deposits, and revenue for 2026, despite isolated credit headwinds, and sees positive trends in core deposit and fee income expansion supporting ongoing shareholder value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NuGen Medical names Ajay Mishra CFO
data.description:
• NuGen Medical Devices (NGMD:CA) on Friday said that Chief Financial Officer and Corporate Secretary Veronique Laberge will resign effective January 31, 2026.
  • The company said Ajay Mishra has been appointed as Chief Financial Officer and Corporate Secretary, effective the same date.
  • NGMD:CA closed +16.66% at $0.035.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: First Hawaiian outlines 3%-4% loan growth and $520M expense target for 2026 amid strong deposit trends
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Hawaiian, Inc. (FHB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Robert Harrison, Chairman, President & CEO, reported that "the state unemployment rate continued to fall and was at 2.2% in November compared to the national unemployment rate of 4.5%." He noted, "We had another strong quarter. Our NIM expanded, net interest income grew, expenses were well contained and credit quality remained strong." Harrison highlighted return on average tangible equity at 15.8% for the fourth quarter and 16.3% for the full year, and shared that the effective tax rate was 24.8% for the quarter due to the reversal of a previously accrued tax benefit, expecting it to return to about 23.2% going forward.
  • Harrison stated, "We continue to be well capitalized with ample liquidity. We had good growth in C&I loans as well as retail and commercial deposits." He announced, "During the quarter, we repurchased about 1 million shares, which used the remaining $26 million of our $100 million purchase authorization for 2025. Our new stock repurchase authorization is for $250 million."
  • James Moses, Vice Chairman of Finance Group & CFO, said, "Retail and commercial deposits increased $233 million, while public deposits declined by $447 million. That dynamic resulted in a net increase in deposits of $214 million in the fourth quarter. The total cost of deposits fell by 9 basis points to 1.29%, and our noninterest-bearing deposit ratio was 32%." Moses added, "Net interest income was $170.3 million, $1 million higher than the prior quarter. The NIM in the fourth quarter was 3.21%, up 2 basis points compared to the prior quarter."
  • Lea Nakamura, Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer, stated, "The bank continues to maintain its strong credit performance and healthy credit metrics. Credit risk remains low, stable and well within our expectations." She highlighted that classified assets decreased by 7 basis points, while special mention assets increased by 16 basis points, and net charge-offs were $5 million for the quarter.

Outlook

  • Harrison projected, "We expect full year loan growth to be 3% to 4% range. The growth will be driven primarily by CRE and C&I loans."
  • He continued, "We anticipate that the full year NIM will be in the 3.16% to 3.18% range."
  • Harrison also stated, "We expect noninterest income to be stable and to come in at about $220 million for the year. And finally, we expect expenses to be about $520 million in 2026."
  • Management expects the effective tax rate to return to about 23.2% going forward.

Financial Results

  • Net interest income for the fourth quarter was $170.3 million and NIM was 3.21%.
  • Noninterest income was $55.6 million.
  • Noninterest expense in the fourth quarter was $125.1 million.
  • Retail and commercial deposits increased $233 million, while public deposits declined by $447 million, resulting in a net increase in deposits of $214 million.
  • The total cost of deposits fell by 9 basis points to 1.29%, with a noninterest-bearing deposit ratio of 32%.
  • Provision for credit losses was $7.7 million, with allowance for credit losses at $168.5 million, and coverage increasing to 118 basis points of total loans and leases.

Q&A

  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about loan growth drivers and outlook for payoffs/paydowns. Harrison explained, "We had some payoffs in the CRE portfolio that we had anticipated to come in later, which is why we didn't quite hit the number we had talked about on the third quarter call." He added, "The pipeline of multifamily is still there, we're very, very busy... we expect the second half of the year to start to see more normalized growth in the CRE on the mainland."
  • Feaster followed up on deposit costs and margin. Moses responded, "I think the margin guide reflects both an ability to continue to cut deposit rates when the Fed cuts, as well as that fixed asset repricing that we continue to talk about... We think the beta is probably going to be a little bit lower go forward than where we were before."
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Sought clarification on fixed cash flows and spread competition. Moses said, "We still think it's 180, 200 basis points on the securities portfolio and that's pretty fixed... maybe 100 basis points, somewhere in 80, 100 basis points pickup on the loans versus the 200 basis points or so on the securities."
  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Asked about deposit beta expectations and expense normalization. Moses confirmed, "the interest-bearing deposit beta is going to step down over time." Harrison attributed past expense control to technology investments and noted, "as we go forward into 2026, we see that most of that we've captured."
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Inquired about buyback pace and capital deployment. Moses said, "we have a pretty good appetite to continue the pace that we had set last year... but we haven't really made any firm commitment... we recognize we have plenty of capital to do any number of things with."
  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked about spot rate on deposits and expense seasonality. Moses reported, "Spot rate on deposits at the end of the year, 1.24% in December."
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan: Asked about deposit balances and NIM guide. Moses said, "Probably... it really will depend on the amount of rate cuts that we see and the timing of those and whether they're 25 or whether they're 50."
  • Timur Braziler, Wells Fargo: Inquired about loan growth sources and C&I yields. Harrison discussed continued pursuit of new opportunities, especially on the West Coast, and provided context on construction-to-CRE loan conversions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for more details on loan growth, deposit costs, and buyback strategy, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they acknowledged strong performance but probed for risks in payoffs and normalization of expenses.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident, with Harrison stating, "We had another strong quarter." During Q&A, management maintained a balanced and transparent approach, acknowledging uncertainties in loan growth timing and expense normalization but expressing confidence in their strategies.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's sentiment shifted from cautious optimism to confident execution, while analysts maintained a consistent, probing tone with a focus on understanding sustainability and future risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more precise, with clear targets for loan growth, NIM, noninterest income, and expenses, compared to broader outlooks in Q3.
  • Strategic focus shifted from maintaining flat investment portfolios and awaiting normalization in C&I to actively targeting CRE and C&I loan growth and expanding buyback authorization.
  • Analysts' questions in both quarters focused on growth drivers and margin sustainability, but in Q4, there was more attention to expense normalization and capital return flexibility.
  • Management's confidence appears to have increased, citing completed technology investments and a solid capital position, while acknowledging timing-related variances in loan payoffs and funding.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted payoffs in the CRE portfolio occurring sooner than expected, citing increased competition from permanent lenders seeking quality assets.
  • Expense growth is projected to normalize after benefiting from technology-driven cost controls, with Harrison emphasizing, "most of that we've captured."
  • There is continued risk from unpredictable deposit flows, particularly from public sector sources.
  • Credit risk remains low, but coverage has been increased, and management is monitoring classified and special mention assets.

Final Takeaway

First Hawaiian delivered another quarter of solid performance, highlighted by expanding net interest margin, disciplined expense management, and robust credit quality. For 2026, the company projects 3%-4% loan growth, stable noninterest income at $220 million, and expenses of about $520 million, while maintaining flexibility with a new $250 million share repurchase authorization. Management remains focused on leveraging its strong capital position, expanding core lending businesses, and navigating a competitive lending market with stable deposit costs and prudent risk management.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Abacus Global Management announces $20M share repurchase program
data.description:
• Abacus Global Management (ABX) announced on Friday that its Board of Directors has authorized a $20M share repurchase program, effective January 30, 2026.
  • Abacus expects to fund the share repurchase program through cash on hand and free cash flow. 
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: OverActive Media announces private placement extension
data.description:
• OverActive Media (OAM:CA) (OTC: OAMCF) said on Friday it has extended its previously announced non-brokered private placement of up to 10 million units priced at $0.30 each, for gross proceeds of up to about $3 million.
  • The company said further closings of the offering are now expected to occur by February 23, 2026, following an initial closing on December 30, 2025, subject to regulatory approval and customary conditions.
  • Each unit consists of one common share and one warrant, with each warrant exercisable at $0.40 for 24 months, subject to acceleration.
  • OAM:CA closed -0.0% at $0.245.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: AZZ Inc. authorizes new $100M share repurchase program
data.description:
• AZZ (AZZ) on Friday said its board approved a new share repurchase program authorizing the company to buy back up to $100 million of its common stock, effective immediately.
  • The new program will run alongside a prior $100 million repurchase authorization approved in 2020, under which about $33.2 million remained available as of November 30, 2025.
  • The company had about 30.0 million shares outstanding at the end of its third quarter of fiscal 2026.
  • AZZ said repurchases may be made in the open market or through private transactions, including under Rule 10b5-1 trading plans.
  • AZZ -0.0% after hours to $124.29
  • Source: Press Release
